text: The idea that cultural and creative industries (CCIs) could contribute to national economic development was first introduced in the UK mapping report (DCMS, Creative industries mapping document. HMSO, 1998) and reaffirmed in the ‘sector deal’ for the creative industries (Bazalgette, Independent review of the creative industries. Department for Media, Culture and Sport, September 22. www.gov.uk/government/publications/independent-review-of-the-creative-industries, 2018) document that accompanied the UK’s Industrial Strategy (BEIS, Industrial strategy: Building a Britain fit for the future. Department for Business, Energy & Industrial Strategy, November 27. Accessible via www.gov.uk/government/publications/industrial-strategy-building-a-britain-fit-for-the-future, 2017). This chapter explores the role of these narratives of CCI policymaking within the context of two city-regional Local Industrial Strategy (LIS) pilots in the West Midlands and the North West of England. Discourse analysis of the LIS pilots provides insight into the social practices and discourses of “the local”, the rhetoric of creativity and the boundary work (Lamont & Molnár, The study of boundaries in the social sciences. Annual Review of Sociology, 28, 167–195. https://doi.org/10.1146/annurevsoc.28.110601.141107, 2002) that are embedded in policy processes. It demonstrates that both city-regional LIS pilots negotiate contractual relations between the national and the local and discursive practices and social interactions that follow Paasi’s (Regional geography in 2020. In A. Kobayashi (Ed.), International encyclopedia of human geography. Elsevier, 2020) characterisation of border-making and thus illustrates the agency of place in policy instruments that aim to support economic growth at a sub-national level.
explanation: The passage uses authentic, domain-specific citations and a coherent academic narrative that align with real policy discourse, making AI authorship less likely. The precise reference style and integration of named sources suggest human authorship.
